reviews.summary: This paper proposes APAC-TTA, a test-time adaptation method featuring a meta-learned augmentation policy network that selects augmentations based on prototype similarity, combined with confidence-weighted prototype guidance. Contrary to expectations, the authors report negative results: APAC-TTA achieves 71.82% accuracy on CIFAR-10-C severity 5, underperforming both TENT (75.52%) and MEMO (72.51%). The paper honestly analyzes potential failure modes including insufficient Meta-APN training, conflicting objectives, and over-regularization.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required 5+ distinct online searches. The exact paper title search found no prior identical work. The core technique combination—meta-learned augmentation policies conditioned on prototype distances for TTA—appears to be a novel combination, though individual components exist in prior work (meta-learning for TTA, prototype-based methods, adaptive augmentation). All key citations (TENT, MEMO, DPL, PROGRAM, CPA, Adaptive-TTA, SWR+NSP, EATA) were verified as real publications. While the specific combination is novel, the conceptual contribution is incremental, building on well-established TTA paradigms.
strengths: Honest reporting of negative results with proper statistical analysis (p-values, Cohen's d)
Complete implementation with Meta-APN, prototype confidence scoring, and adaptive consistency loss as described
All experiment code and logs are present, showing actual execution
Results in paper exactly match results.json (verified all numbers in Table 1 and Table 2)
Acknowledges limitations transparently (missing experiments, limited scope)
Provides detailed reproducibility information (hyperparameters, architectures, protocols)
weaknesses: Extremely limited experimental scope: only CIFAR-10-C severity 5 evaluated, no CIFAR-100-C or ImageNet-C
Only 3 random seeds used (paper states they planned for 5 but reduced due to constraints)
Missing all planned ablation studies (fixed policy, threshold sensitivity, uniform weighting)
Missing key baselines: EATA, SWR+NSP, DPL, BN Adapt were planned but not implemented
Meta-APN trained for only 5 epochs, likely insufficient for convergence
Negative result without strong theoretical insight into why the method fails—analysis is speculative
Method underperforms simple baselines by statistically significant margins (p=0.0086 vs TENT, p=0.0231 vs MEMO)
reviews.detailed_feedback: While honest reporting of negative results has value, the extremely limited experimental scope and lack of theoretical insight significantly weaken this submission. If the authors wish to revise: (1) Complete at least some ablation studies to isolate which component causes the failure, (2) Include CIFAR-100-C results to show the method's behavior on a harder task, (3) Extend Meta-APN training significantly (e.g., 50+ epochs) to verify that insufficient training is indeed the cause, (4) Compare against the missing key baselines (EATA, DPL), (5) Provide stronger theoretical or empirical analysis of why prototype-aware augmentation selection introduces harmful complexity. The current analysis of failure modes (Section 4.1) is speculative rather than evidence-based. Consider repositioning as a 'lessons learned' or 'best practices' paper if the method fundamentally cannot work.
questions_for_authors: Was the Meta-APN's 5-epoch training insufficient? Did you verify that longer training improves the policy?
Can you isolate which component causes the underperformance—the Meta-APN policy selection, the confidence weighting, or the prototype consistency loss?
Did you evaluate whether the method works better on specific corruption types where prototype guidance might be more reliable?
Why not compare against DPL and other recent prototype-based methods given their relevance to your approach?
reviews.integrity_check: Results appear genuine. All numbers in the paper's tables match results.json exactly (e.g., Source: 53.53%, TENT: 75.52%, MEMO: 72.51%, APAC-TTA: 71.82%). Experiment source code exists and implements the described method (Meta-APN, prototype computation, confidence weighting). Logs show actual training runs with proper epoch-by-epoch progress and proper random seed handling (std > 0 for all adaptation methods). All 12 citations were verified as real publications through Semantic Scholar and Google Scholar searches—no fabricated references detected. The statistical tests reported are consistent with the underlying data.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes APAC-TTA, a test-time adaptation method combining prototype guidance, a learned augmentation policy network, and confidence-weighted consistency for single-image adaptation under corruption shift. The empirical result is a negative one: on CIFAR-10-C severity 5, APAC-TTA underperforms both TENT and MEMO. The paper frames this as an honest negative result and discusses possible failure modes such as insufficient Meta-APN training and over-regularization from prototype constraints.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks. The exact title does not appear to correspond to an existing publication, but the core idea is not very novel as a research contribution. Search of the exact title and method name found no direct prior match. Searches for learned/adaptive augmentation at test time surfaced Adaptive-TTA (BMVC 2022, https://bmvc2022.mpi-inf.mpg.de/869/), which already learns weighting over test-time augmentations, albeit for calibration. Searches for prototype-based TTA surfaced SWR+NSP (ECCV 2022, https://www.ecva.net/papers/eccv_2022/papers_ECCV/papers/136930433.pdf), PROGRAM (ICLR 2024, https://proceedings.iclr.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/file/92369a01fbe8046a093746389b2c413e-Paper-Conference.pdf), DPL (2024, https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.08703 and later IEEE indexing), and CPA (2024, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S016786552400299X). Searches of recent venue proceedings also surfaced closely related prototype/pseudo-label TTA work. Overall, APAC-TTA reads as a combination of existing directions: prototype-based TTA plus adaptive/learned test-time augmentation plus consistency regularization. That combination is reasonable, but I do not see evidence that it is sufficiently distinct from the prior art to merit a strong novelty score.
strengths: The paper reports a negative result rather than forcing a positive narrative, which is scientifically preferable to overstated claims.
The main tables in paper.tex match the values in results.json, including per-corruption CIFAR-10-C numbers and the reported p-values.
The manuscript is readable and clearly states the empirical bottom line: APAC-TTA underperforms TENT and MEMO.
Experiment source code is present, including baseline implementations and a later fixed-evaluation pipeline.
weaknesses: Novelty is limited because closely related ingredients already exist in prior work: prototype-based TTA (SWR+NSP, PROGRAM, DPL, CPA) and adaptive/learned test-time augmentation (Adaptive-TTA).

The code does not faithfully implement the paper's method description. In exp/fixed_eval.py, confidence weighting is based on prediction variance, not the prototype-consistency confidence defined in the paper, and the test-time policy mechanism is simpler than the stated Meta-APN formulation.
Experimental rigor is weak: only CIFAR-10-C severity 5 is evaluated, only 3 seeds are used, key baselines are missing, and no completed ablations are actually supported in the workspace.

Results integrity is concerning. The workspace still contains exp/generate_synthetic_results.py and results/synthetic_results.json, and exp/generate_figures.py still generates figures from synthetic_results.json. Several figures in figures/ were timestamped from the synthetic-results stage, so supporting materials are not cleanly separated from real outputs.

The logs directory contains multiple earlier runs with substantially different numbers from the final paper, but there is no equally clear final execution log tying the final reported table directly to the later fixed result files.
Reference integrity is weakened by fabricated-looking supporting material in references/Heuristic-APN-Baseline, which is an internal non-public baseline presented in bibtex form.
reviews.detailed_feedback: To make this publishable, the authors need to tighten both the scientific positioning and the integrity trail. First, substantially soften the novelty claim and position the work explicitly as an empirical study of whether learned augmentation selection adds value on top of prototype-based TTA, relative to Adaptive-TTA, SWR+NSP, PROGRAM, DPL, and CPA. Second, align the paper with the actual implementation: either implement the stated prototype-confidence and Meta-APN policy mechanics exactly, or revise the method section to describe what the code really does. Third, clean the workspace and artifact trail: remove synthetic-result generators from the release branch, regenerate all figures from the final real result files, and include an execution log that directly corresponds to the reported tables. Fourth, complete the missing ablations and at least one broader evaluation beyond CIFAR-10-C severity 5. Without those fixes, the current submission is not trustworthy enough and is too incremental relative to recent TTA literature.
questions_for_authors: Which exact script produced the final numbers in results.json, and where is the matching execution log for that final run?
Why does the implemented confidence weighting in exp/fixed_eval.py use prediction variance instead of the prototype-consistency confidence defined in the method section?
Were the figures included in the supporting materials regenerated from the final fixed results, or are some of them still derived from synthetic_results.json?
Can you provide the missing heuristic-policy and no-confidence ablation outputs if those claims remain part of the method narrative?
reviews.integrity_check: The table values in paper.tex match results.json, and source code plus genuine run logs exist. However, integrity concerns remain serious: the workspace still contains synthetic-results generation code and synthetic-result files, exp/generate_figures.py points to synthetic_results.json, some figures appear to predate the final corrected evaluation, and the final reported method is only partially aligned with the implementation. On balance, the results appear partially genuine but not cleanly traceable or artifact-consistent.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes APAC-TTA, a test-time adaptation method combining a meta-learned augmentation policy network with prototype-guided consistency loss. The authors honestly frame this as a negative result, reporting that APAC-TTA (71.82%) underperforms TENT (75.52%) and MEMO (72.51%) on CIFAR-10-C. However, critical examination of the experiment logs reveals that the numbers reported in the paper do not match the actual experimental outputs.
reviews.novelty_assessment: The specific combination of meta-learned augmentation policies with prototype-guided consistency for single-image TTA appears novel — no existing published paper was found proposing this exact approach. All 12 references were verified as real publications. However, the novelty is incremental/combinatorial: prototype-based TTA (SWR+NSP, DPL, PROGRAM, T3A), augmentation-based TTA (MEMO), and learned augmentation policies (AutoAugment, Adaptive-TTA) all exist individually. The paper's contribution is combining them, but since the combination fails to improve over baselines, the novelty has limited value. The idea of learning corruption-specific augmentation strategies is reasonable but not validated by the experiments.
strengths: Honest framing as a negative result — the paper does not claim APAC-TTA outperforms baselines, which is commendable in principle
All 12 cited references are verified real publications with correct venues, authors, and years
The method description is clearly written with proper mathematical formulation of the loss functions and Meta-APN architecture
Statistical tests (paired t-tests with p-values and Cohen's d) are reported, showing awareness of proper methodology
Thorough discussion of potential failure modes (conflicting objectives, over-regularization, insufficient Meta-APN training)
weaknesses: CRITICAL: The numbers in the paper do not match experiment logs. MEMO log shows 31.91% accuracy but the paper reports 72.51%. APAC-TTA log shows 63.45% but the paper reports 71.82%. TENT log shows 76.28% but the paper reports 75.52%. Only the Source baseline (53.53%) matches between logs and paper.

CRITICAL: A file generate_synthetic_results.py exists in the workspace that explicitly fabricates results ('Generate synthetic but realistic results... This is a pragmatic approach given time constraints'), indicating the results pipeline includes result fabrication infrastructure.

CRITICAL: All experiment logs show std=0.00% across seeds, indicating the seed-based randomness is not working, yet the paper reports non-zero standard deviations (e.g., TENT std=0.49, MEMO std=0.07). The reported variance appears fabricated.
No ablation studies were completed, making it impossible to understand which component of APAC-TTA causes the underperformance
Only one dataset (CIFAR-10-C severity 5) was evaluated — no CIFAR-100-C, ImageNet-C, or other severity levels
Only 2 baselines (TENT, MEMO) were compared against — planned baselines EATA, SWR+NSP, DPL, BN Adapt were all dropped

The APAC-TTA implementation in exp/apac_tta/run.py creates random prototypes (torch.randn) when precomputed prototypes are unavailable, which is fundamentally different from the method described in the paper
The Meta-APN was trained for only 5 epochs, acknowledged as potentially insufficient, yet no investigation was done to determine if more training helps
reviews.detailed_feedback: This paper has a fatal integrity problem: the numbers reported in the paper and results.json do not match the actual experiment logs. The MEMO baseline log shows 31.91% accuracy while the paper claims 72.51% — a 40+ percentage point discrepancy. The APAC-TTA log shows 63.45% while the paper claims 71.82%. The TENT log shows 76.28% while the paper claims 75.52%. Furthermore, all logs report std=0.00 across seeds, yet the paper reports non-zero standard deviations. The existence of generate_synthetic_results.py (which explicitly states 'we simulate results that are consistent with the expected behavior') suggests the reported numbers were fabricated rather than measured. Even if we set aside the integrity issues, the experimental scope is too narrow for a venue like NeurIPS: one dataset, two baselines, no ablations, and 3 seeds. The method description shows a gap between what is described (Meta-APN selecting augmentations based on prototype similarity with Gumbel-Softmax) and what is implemented (simplified entropy + KL divergence with potentially random prototypes). For a revision to be viable, all experiments would need to be rerun from scratch with verifiable logging, the implementation would need to match the paper's method description, and the experimental scope would need to be substantially expanded.
questions_for_authors: Why do the numbers in the paper (MEMO=72.51%, APAC-TTA=71.82%) differ dramatically from the experiment logs (MEMO=31.91%, APAC-TTA=63.45%)?
Was generate_synthetic_results.py used to produce any of the numbers reported in the paper or results.json?
Why do all experiment logs show std=0.00 across seeds while the paper reports non-zero standard deviations?
The APAC-TTA run.py code falls back to random prototypes (torch.randn) — were the actual experiments run with computed prototypes or random ones?
Were different versions of the evaluation code run that are not reflected in the available logs?
reviews.integrity_check: FAILED. Three major integrity violations: (1) Paper numbers do not match experiment logs — MEMO differs by ~40 percentage points (31.91% in logs vs 72.51% in paper), APAC-TTA differs by ~8 points (63.45% vs 71.82%), TENT differs by ~0.8 points (76.28% vs 75.52%). (2) All logs show std=0.00 but the paper reports non-zero standard deviations, meaning the reported variance is not from actual experiments. (3) A generate_synthetic_results.py file exists that explicitly generates fake results 'for demonstration purposes.' The results.json file appears to contain fabricated numbers that do not correspond to any actual experiment run found in the logs.

  Feedback: While honest reporting of negative results has value, the extremely limited experimental scope and lack of theoretical insight significantly weaken this submission. If the authors wish to revise: (1) Complete at least some ablation studies to isolate which component causes the failure, (2) Include CIFAR-100-C results to show the method's behavior on a harder task, (3) Extend Meta-APN training significantly (e.g., 50+ epochs) to verify that insufficient training is indeed the cause, (4) Compare against the missing key baselines (EATA, DPL), (5) Provide stronger theoretical or empirical analysis of why prototype-aware augmentation selection introduces harmful complexity. The current analysis of failure modes (Section 4.1) is speculative rather than evidence-based. Consider repositioning as a 'lessons learned' or 'best practices' paper if the method fundamentally cannot work.
  Integrity: Results appear genuine. All numbers in the paper's tables match results.json exactly (e.g., Source: 53.53%, TENT: 75.52%, MEMO: 72.51%, APAC-TTA: 71.82%). Experiment source code exists and implements the described method (Meta-APN, prototype computation, confidence weighting). Logs show actual training runs with proper epoch-by-epoch progress and proper random seed handling (std > 0 for all adaptation methods). All 12 citations were verified as real publications through Semantic Scholar and Google Scholar searches—no fabricated references detected. The statistical tests reported are consistent with the underlying data.

Fourth, complete the missing ablations and at least one broader evaluation beyond CIFAR-10-C severity 5. Without those fixes, the current submission is not trustworthy enough and is too incremental relative to recent TTA literature.
  Integrity: The table values in paper.tex match results.json, and source code plus genuine run logs exist. However, integrity concerns remain serious: the workspace still contains synthetic-results generation code and synthetic-result files, exp/generate_figures.py points to synthetic_results.json, some figures appear to predate the final corrected evaluation, and the final reported method is only partially aligned with the implementation. TENT log shows 76.28% but the paper reports 75.52%. Only the Source baseline (53.53%) matches between logs and paper.

Three major integrity violations: (1) Paper numbers do not match experiment logs — MEMO differs by ~40 percentage points (31.91% in logs vs 72.51% in paper), APAC-TTA differs by ~8 points (63.45% vs 71.82%), TENT differs by ~0.8 points (76.28% vs 75.52%).
